Every place, every object, every garment has as a character.
I reveal character  through editorial storytelling.

Every place leaves something behind.

Observation always comes before the camera.

Tone explains the feeling before we feel.

Good branding begins with paying attention.

Spaces are remembered through feeling, not furniture.

Every object belongs to a larger story.

Architecture isn't static. It changes with memory.

Atmosphere is designed long before it's photographed.

Identity is built through repetition.

Sometimes the smallest detail carries the entire narrative.

About
—
Rebecca Hanna (Bex)

New York  ·  Cairo  ·  @videogyal

About

I don't begin with a camera. I begin with observation. Every place already has a character. Every object carries memory. Every garment holds intention. My work is about noticing those qualities and translating them into editorial narratives that feel emotionally connected to the world they come from.

My background in branding taught me that identity isn't something you invent. It's something you uncover. Whether I'm working with a hotel, a fashion collection, an interior, or a neighborhood, I'm interested in the details that quietly shape how people remember a place. Those details become the story.

Philosophy

Branding isn't just visual. It's emotional. It's spatial. It's cultural. The strongest identities aren't created by adding more. They're revealed by paying closer attention.

Every place already tells us who it is. The architecture. The materials. The light. The rituals. The sounds. The objects. The people who pass through. My role is to translate those qualities into editorial campaigns that feel authentic to the identity already present.

I don't document places. I reveal their character.

Videogyal
videogyal (noun)

A girl who observes the character of places, objects, garments, and culture through editorial storytelling. A girl who studies atmosphere before aesthetics, identity before imagery, and observation before creation.
